votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the prime check, list product, and the factorization specification checking all conditions: n >= 1, sortedness, primality of factors, and product equality.
The Python code correctly implements the primality test, list product, and the factorization specification, checking all conditions exactly as defined in the Coq code.
The Python code correctly implements the primality test, list product, and the factorization specification, matching the Coq definitions on all concrete inputs.

The Python code correctly implements all components: is_prime checks primality (the optimization using sqrt is mathematically equivalent to checking all d from 2 to p-1), list_prod computes the product of a list, and factorize_spec checks all four conditions (n >= 1, sorted in non-decreasing order, all elements prime, product equals n). The Python implementations will return the same boolean judgments as the Coq spec for all concrete inputs.

The Python implementations will return the same boolean judgments as the Coq Prop definitions for all concrete inputs.

Python checks n>=1, nondecreasing order, primes (>=2) via correct trial division, and product equals n; list_prod and sortedness match Coq, so results align on all inputs.

Python implements the same primality predicate, list product, and checks for n>=1, non-decreasing order, primality of all factors, and product equality; adjacent <= checks suffice for Sorted Z.le. Behavior matches on all concrete inputs.

is_prime matches the Coq definition for all integers (>=2 prime iff no divisors in [2,p)), list_prod computes the same product with identity 1, and factorize_spec enforces n>=1, nondecreasing order, primality of factors, and exact product; adjacency check suffices for Sorted with <=.
